title: Hypertension
subtitle: Learning Journey
description: Master the complete path from mechanism to diagnosis, drug selection, and monitoring.

nodes.title: The Beginning
nodes.summary: Hypertension is a silent but high-impact disease best learned as a connected story.
nodes.hint: Start with the big picture before details.

bullets: Often asymptomatic until damage develops
Major driver of cardiovascular and renal risk
Best learned in sequence, not as isolated facts

nodes.title: Big Picture
nodes.summary: Hypertension progressively injures target organs and raises long-term cardiovascular risk.
nodes.hint: Common + silent + harmful.

bullets: Targets the heart, brain, kidneys, and vessels
Risk rises as blood pressure rises
Control reduces long-term complications

nodes.title: Definition & Types
nodes.summary: Most cases are primary hypertension, while secondary hypertension has an identifiable cause.
nodes.hint: Primary = common. Secondary = search for the cause.

left.title: Primary
items: Usually linked to multiple risk factors

right.title: Secondary
items: Think renal, endocrine, sleep apnea, drugs
More suspicious in sudden or unusual cases

nodes.title: Risk Factors
nodes.summary: Lifestyle and inherited factors work together to increase the chance of hypertension.
nodes.hint: Think lifestyle + genetics + metabolic disease.

bullets: Obesity, high sodium intake, and low activity
Smoking, alcohol excess, diabetes, and dyslipidemia
Family history and increasing age

nodes.title: Pathophysiology Boss
nodes.summary: Blood pressure rises when cardiac output, vascular resistance, or both are increased through neurohormonal dysregulation.
nodes.hint: HTN = vessels + heart under SNS and RAAS control.

notes: SNS raises heart rate, contractility, and vessel tone
RAAS raises vasoconstriction and sodium/water retention
Most drug classes target one or both pathways

nodes.title: RAAS Branch
nodes.summary: RAAS drives vasoconstriction and volume expansion through angiotensin II and aldosterone.
nodes.hint: RAAS mainly increases volume and vascular tone.

bullets: Renin activates the angiotensin cascade
Angiotensin II increases vasoconstriction
Aldosterone increases sodium and water retention

nodes.title: SNS Branch
nodes.summary: The sympathetic nervous system increases blood pressure through alpha and beta receptor stimulation.
nodes.hint: SNS = tighter vessels + stronger heart.

bullets: Alpha-1 stimulation increases vasoconstriction
Beta-1 stimulation increases heart rate and contractility
Overall effect: higher SVR and cardiac output

nodes.title: Diagnosis Gate
nodes.summary: Diagnosis needs repeated accurate readings and should not rely on one office value alone.
nodes.hint: One office reading is never enough.

nodes.title: Classification
nodes.summary: BP stage guides treatment intensity and urgency.
nodes.hint: Normal → Elevated → Stage 1 → Stage 2.

nodes.title: Lifestyle First
nodes.summary: Lifestyle modification is foundational in prevention and treatment of hypertension.
nodes.hint: Lifestyle is not optional; it is part of treatment.

bullets: Weight loss, DASH diet, and sodium reduction
Regular physical activity and limited alcohol
Smoking cessation and broader CV risk control

nodes.title: When to Start Drugs
nodes.summary: Drug therapy depends on BP stage, cardiovascular risk, and overall clinical context.
nodes.hint: Higher stage = more likely to need medication.

steps.title: Stage 1 HTN
steps.value: Lifestyle ± medication depending on overall risk

steps.title: Markedly High BP
steps.value: Often needs stronger or combination approach

nodes.title: First-Line Agents
nodes.summary: Most patients begin therapy with one of the major first-line antihypertensive families.
nodes.hint: Learn the big families first before the details.

nodes.title: ACEIs & ARBs
nodes.summary: ACE inhibitors and ARBs block RAAS and are especially useful in selected kidney-related high-risk patients.
nodes.hint: RAAS blockers = kidney protection in the right patient, but no pregnancy.

content.snapshot: Core RAAS blockers used widely in hypertension and kidney-protective settings.

sections.label: Mechanism
sections.value: Reduce angiotensin II signaling and lower vasoconstriction plus aldosterone effect.

sections.label: Best Use Cases
items: Patients where RAAS blockade is preferred

sections.label: Contraindications / Cautions
items: Use caution in renal artery stenosis

sections.label: Pearl
sections.value: ARBs usually avoid the cough problem seen with ACE inhibitors.

nodes.title: Thiazides
nodes.summary: Thiazide-type diuretics are strong first-line options that lower BP mainly through sodium and water reduction.
nodes.hint: Thiazides = volume down, potassium down.

content.snapshot: Common first-line drugs with strong BP-lowering value in many patients.

sections.label: Mechanism
sections.value: Promote sodium and water loss, lowering extracellular volume and contributing to lower blood pressure.

sections.label: Best Use Cases
items: Often strong option in Black patients

sections.label: Pearl
sections.value: Chlorthalidone is often preferred over HCTZ.

nodes.title: Calcium Channel Blockers
nodes.summary: Calcium channel blockers lower pressure by reducing vascular tone or slowing cardiac conduction depending on subtype.
nodes.hint: DHP = vessels. Non-DHP = heart.

right.title: Non-DHP CCBs
items: More effect on heart rate and conduction
Different clinical role from DHP agents
Need more caution in some cardiac situations

nodes.title: Beta-Blockers
nodes.summary: Beta-blockers are not routine first-line drugs for uncomplicated hypertension but are useful in selected patients.
nodes.hint: Special-situation drugs, not universal first-line.

content.snapshot: Useful when hypertension overlaps with other cardiovascular needs.

sections.label: Mechanism
sections.value: Reduce heart rate and contractility, lowering cardiac output.

sections.label: Caution
items: Use carefully in selected patients with reactive airway issues

sections.label: Pearl
sections.value: Not usually first-line for uncomplicated hypertension alone.

nodes.title: Combination Therapy
nodes.summary: Many patients, especially with stage 2 hypertension, need more than one medication to reach target blood pressure.
nodes.hint: Combine smart, not random.

steps.title: Need More Control
steps.value: Consider adding a second complementary agent

steps.title: Avoid
steps.value: Do not combine ACEI with ARB

nodes.title: Special Populations
nodes.summary: Drug choice should be individualized according to comorbidities, kidney status, ethnicity, and pregnancy status.
nodes.hint: Treat the patient, not the number alone.

groups.label: Many Black Patients
items: Thiazides and CCBs often respond well

groups.label: Special Situations
items: CAD or arrhythmias may favor beta-blockers

nodes.title: Monitoring
nodes.summary: Antihypertensive therapy needs follow-up for both effectiveness and safety.
nodes.hint: Monitoring = BP + kidneys + potassium + side effects.

nodes.title: Drug Pearls
nodes.summary: A few medication pearls prevent many common hypertension mistakes.
nodes.hint: Pearls protect you from common prescribing and counseling mistakes.

cards: ACEIs and ARBs are contraindicated in pregnancy
Chlorthalidone is often preferred to HCTZ

nodes.title: Mastery Achieved
nodes.summary: You now have the full hypertension journey from mechanism to personalized drug strategy.
nodes.hint: Mechanism → diagnosis → stage → treatment → monitoring.

bullets: Recognize the major pathways
Choose drugs by mechanism and patient factors
Monitor response and adverse effects with purpose
